content.text:
<timestamp>Friday, Jun 19, 2026, 8:41 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>
<user_query>
IPD  - 1 Should we implement our own product?
THE PROBLEM
Context
On review, most requirements in Legal Contract Intelligence Platform overlap with capabilities mature CLM vendors already ship well: document storage, full-text and natural-language search, extraction, and document-to-document linking. The only gap is, reconstructing the originating context of a contract from our Zammad/Jira ticketing systems.)

Problem Statement
Should we buy any CLM and integrate into our intake system or build our own legal contract intelligence product?
OPTIONS CONSIDERED

Buy Sirion + build a ticket-origin integration layer [Selected]
Use Sirion for contract intelligence and repository capabilities. Build a lightweight integration service to connect Zammad/Jira context into the contract lifecycle.
Build a custom contract intelligence platform from scratch
Develop repository, extraction, retrieval, and relationship management capabilities internally.
Buy LinkSquares + build a ticket-origin integration layer
Use LinkSquares as the CLM foundation and extend it with custom integrations for ticket-origin tracking.

Among these which would you suggest
</user_query>

Let me read the problem statement document to understand the full context before giving a recommendation.

I need to review the problem statement file to understand the context around this build-versus-buy decision for a Legal Contract Intelligence Platform before offering a recommendation.

Note: the `problem_statement.md` in this workspace is about a different product (a Cursor coaching tool), so I'm answering purely from the IPD context you pasted.

## Short answer

**Go with a "buy + thin integration layer" approach — and Sirion is the right default choice (Option 1), provided one POC condition checks out.** The build-from-scratch option (Option 2) should be ruled out almost immediately. Between the two buy options, Sirion vs. LinkSquares comes down to fit, not feasibility.

## Why Option 2 (build from scratch) is the weakest

Your own framing kills it: you've stated that **~90% of the requirements are commodity capabilities that mature CLM vendors already ship well**, and the *only* real gap is ticket-origin context reconstruction. Building from scratch means:

- Spending the vast majority of your effort rebuilding undifferentiated commodity features (storage, full-text/NL search, extraction, doc-to-doc linking).
- Owning forever the cost, security, compliance (legal data is sensitive), and AI-model maintenance burden.
- Producing zero differentiation, because the one thing that *is* differentiated (ticket-origin linkage) is exactly the small piece you'd build in *either* buy option anyway.

Build-from-scratch is only justified when the market can't meet core needs, or you have unique scale/data-sovereignty/cost constraints. None of those are present here. **Rule it out.**

## Sirion vs. LinkSquares — the decision that actually matters

Since the only thing you're truly building is the integration layer, the decisive criteria are: **(a) fit to your contract complexity, (b) integration/API extensibility, and (c) cost-to-value.**

| Criterion | Sirion | LinkSquares |
|---|---|---|
| Core strength | AI-native contract *intelligence* + full lifecycle (incl. strong post-signature obligation/risk mgmt) | Fast-deploy repository + extraction, in-house legal team focus |
| Best fit for | Complex, high-volume, high-risk contracting (buy + sell side) | Mid-market, faster time-to-value, lighter footprint |
| Extraction / intelligence depth | Deeper, more configurable | Strong, more turnkey |
| Implementation cost & time | Higher, longer | Lower, faster |
| API/integration openness | Enterprise-grade APIs (verify webhook/event support) | Good APIs, simpler surface |

**My recommendation: Sirion**, because:
1. "Contract *intelligence* platform" is the stated goal — Sirion is positioned more strongly on intelligence and lifecycle depth, which gives you more headroom as scope grows beyond the initial repository/search/extraction needs.
2. The bet is long-term; if contract complexity or volume is non-trivial, Sirion's depth avoids a re-platforming later.

**It flips to LinkSquares if:** your contract portfolio is relatively standard, your priority is fast time-to-value and lower TCO, and you don't need heavy post-signature obligation management. In that case LinkSquares gives you 80% of the value at materially lower cost and effort.

## The one thing to validate before committing (the real risk)

Both buy options are only as good as the **integration layer's feasibility** — that's where 100% of your differentiation lives. Before finalizing Sirion, confirm in a short POC:

- Does Sirion expose **APIs/webhooks rich enough** to bidirectionally link a contract to its originating Zammad/Jira ticket, and to surface that context inside the CLM UI (or yours)?
- Can you write the **ticket-origin metadata back** into Sirion's data model (custom fields, relationships) cleanly?
- What's the **TCO delta** (license + implementation) vs. LinkSquares for *your* contract volume?

If Sirion's API can't cleanly support the ticket-origin linkage but LinkSquares' can, that single fact should override the capability-depth argument — because the integration is the only part that's uniquely yours.

**Bottom line:** Endorse Option 1 (Buy Sirion + integration layer) as the default, treat LinkSquares as a strong lower-cost fallback, and discard build-from-scratch. Make the final Sirion-vs-LinkSquares call on the POC integration test and TCO, not on brand.

I notice the problem_statement.md file is about a different product entirely—a feedback tool for Cursor conversations—so it's not relevant to this Legal Contract Intelligence Platform decision. The user is asking for a recommendation between three options, which is a straightforward advisory question that doesn't require external tools.

Looking at the actual decision: most of the platform's requirements are already commodity capabilities that mature CLM vendors handle well—document storage, search, extraction, linking. The only real gap is integrating with Zammad/Jira to reconstruct a contract's originating context. This strongly favors a buy-plus-integration approach over building from scratch, since building everything would mean reinventing commodity features with no differentiation. Between Sirion and LinkSquares, I'm weighing their strengths: Sirion is enterprise-grade and AI-native with strong contract intelligence and obligation management, though heavier and more expensive with longer implementation.  LinkSquares is more mid-market, faster to deploy, lighter, and cheaper, with solid AI extraction and repository features. The decisive factor here is integration extensibility—API quality and openness matter most for building that ticket-origin layer. I should give an honest recommendation rather than just validate their Sirion selection, considering cost-to-capability fit and which vendor's APIs will actually support the custom integration work cleanly.

<timestamp>Friday, Jun 19, 2026, 8:44 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>
<user_query>

input.search_term: Sirion CLM pricing cost per year 2026 enterprise
input.explanation: Find Sirion CLM pricing to compare costs

input.search_term: LinkSquares pricing cost per year 2026 CLM
input.explanation: Find LinkSquares pricing to compare costs

content.text:
Here's the money view. CLM pricing is custom/opaque, so these are benchmark ranges from public 2026 data plus standard build-cost estimates — treat them as planning figures to validate in quotes, not exact quotes.

## Per-option cost breakdown

**Option 1 — Buy Sirion + integration layer**

| Cost component | Estimate |
|---|---|
| Annual license | $120k–$300k/yr |
| Implementation (one-time) | $100k–$250k+ |
| Support & maintenance | 18–22% of license/yr |
| Ticket-origin integration build (one-time) | ~$80k–$200k |
| Integration maintenance | ~$50k–$100k/yr |

**Option 3 — Buy LinkSquares + integration layer**

| Cost component | Estimate |
|---|---|
| Annual license | ~$30k–$120k/yr (median ~$31k; enterprise >$75k) |
| Implementation (one-time) | $25k–$100k |
| **API access add-on** (needed for your integration) | ~$10k/yr |
| Add-ons / premium support | +20–40% of license |
| Ticket-origin integration build (one-time) | ~$80k–$200k |
| Integration maintenance | ~$50k–$100k/yr |

**Option 2 — Build from scratch**

| Cost component | Estimate |
|---|---|
| Initial build (5–8 eng + ML, 12–18 mo) | ~$1.5M–$3M+ |
| Ongoing eng + infra + AI/model costs | ~$750k–$1.5M/yr |
| Implementation | $0 (you are the implementer) |

## 3-year total cost of ownership (apples-to-apples)

| Option | 3-yr TCO (rough) |
|---|---|
| **LinkSquares + integration** | **~$0.4M–$1.0M** |
| **Sirion + integration** | **~$1.0M–$2.2M** |
| **Build from scratch** | **~$3M–$6M+** |

## What the money says

- **Build-from-scratch is 3–6× more expensive** than either buy option over 3 years, and you'd be spending that premium to rebuild commodity features — not the differentiated ticket-origin layer (which you build in *all three* options anyway). Money confirms what the strategy already said: rule it out.
- **LinkSquares is materially cheaper than Sirion** — roughly **half the 3-year TCO** at comparable scope. The catch: LinkSquares charges **API access (~$10k/yr) and integration connectors as add-ons**, and your entire value-add depends on that API. So budget the API tier explicitly — it's not optional for you.
- **Sirion costs more because you're paying for depth** (post-signature obligation management, deeper AI/intelligence). The question is whether you'll use that depth. If your need is genuinely "repository + search + extraction + linking + ticket-origin," you may be overpaying for capability you won't exploit.

## Money-driven recommendation

On **cost alone, LinkSquares wins** — meaningfully lower TCO for the stated (largely commodity) requirement set.

This partially revises my earlier "Sirion by default" call: I recommended Sirion on *capability depth*, but your own framing says the requirements are mostly commodity + one integration gap. If that's true, **you're paying a ~$0.6M–$1.2M premium over 3 years for Sirion depth you may not need.**

So the real decision reduces to one question:

**Is your contract portfolio complex/high-volume/high-risk enough to need Sirion's intelligence and obligation-management depth?**
- **Yes →** Sirion justifies its premium.
- **No (it's mostly storage/search/extraction/linking) →** **LinkSquares** is the financially correct choice — just confirm its API can support the Zammad/Jira ticket-origin linkage before signing, and price the API tier in.

Want me to turn this into a one-page TCO comparison sheet (with your actual contract volume and user count plugged in) so it's quote-ready?

Now I'm working through the total cost of ownership over three years, factoring in integration costs for both purchase options alongside the build-from-scratch alternative. Building internally would require a substantial engineering team—roughly five to eight engineers plus machine learning expertise—working for twelve to eighteen months to achieve feature parity, then ongoing maintenance costs beyond that.

For the integration layer approach with either tool, I'm estimating a leaner investment: one to two engineers for two to four months of development plus annual maintenance. Then there's the vendor pricing itself—Sirion in the enterprise range, LinkSquares with its tiered licensing and important API access add-on that factors into the integration costs. I'm thinking through whether to present this as a structured comparison table or canvas given the multiple cost scenarios and assumptions involved. I'm deciding against the canvas approach—this is a quick follow-up in an ongoing conversation, and the workspace isn't clearly a React project. A markdown table with cost figures will be clearer and faster. I'm pulling together the key pricing data for Sirion, LinkSquares, and a build-from-scratch option, then calculating the three-year total cost of ownership for each to give a direct money comparison.

<timestamp>Saturday, Jun 20, 2026, 9:04 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>
<user_query>
Ok I want it for my own company and i am going to implement everything myself using Cursor and Claude Code,It will be used by myself and the members of the company around 40 members .you are suiggesting me to go ith sirion and building a layer on top of it and it will be of lesser cost and saying for implementation I will require a lot of money but i dont think it works that way since using cursor I just need less money, Maybe the time taken would be lesser for considering it on a long time as a owner I want the product which shld be cutomised by myself and it shld increase my revenue not spending the money earnt on API 
Based on these facts give me the best approach
</user_query>

You're right to push back — and your new facts genuinely flip my earlier recommendation. Let me update honestly, because the assumptions behind "buy Sirion" no longer hold.

## Why my earlier advice no longer applies

My earlier numbers assumed: (a) you'd hire an engineering team at market labor rates, and (b) you're an enterprise needing enterprise-grade SLAs. Both are now false:

- **You're building with Cursor + Claude Code**, so labor cost collapses from "$1.5M team" to "your time + AI subscriptions."
- **It's 40 internal users, not an enterprise rollout.** Sirion/LinkSquares price for big orgs — at 40 seats you'd pay enterprise rates for capability and reliability you don't need.
- **You want ownership + customization + revenue**, not a recurring fee that drains earnings.

At *your* scale and with *your* tooling, **build is the right call.** I was wrong to default to buy for your situation.

## The one myth I have to correct (cross-question)

You said you don't want to "spend the money earned on API." Here's the trap: **building your own contract intelligence does NOT eliminate API costs — it just changes who you pay.**

A CLM's "intelligence" (extraction, semantic search, Q&A) runs on LLM calls. If you build it, *you* pay Claude/OpenAI API per document instead of paying Sirion a license. So the real question isn't "API cost vs. no API cost" — it's:

> **Is my own LLM API + hosting bill smaller than a CLM license?**

At 40 users with modest contract volume — **yes, dramatically.** But only if you design it right (see cost controls below). If you naively call an LLM on every search, the API bill can balloon. The good news: that's avoidable.

## Best approach: Build — but *assemble*, don't write from scratch

Don't literally build everything from zero. The smartest money/time move is **proven open-source foundations + a thin custom layer (your real value) + LLM API for intelligence.** Cursor assembles the glue.

| Capability | How to get it (cheap) | Why not from scratch |
|---|---|---|
| Document storage + repository | Postgres + object storage (S3/local) | Solved problem, don't reinvent |
| Full-text + semantic search | **pgvector** (embeddings in Postgres) | Avoids per-query LLM calls = cheap |
| Extraction (parties, dates, clauses) | LLM API **once at ingestion**, cache the result | Run once, store forever — controls cost |
| Doc-to-doc linking | Embeddings similarity + metadata | Standard |
| **Ticket-origin from Zammad/Jira** | **Your custom layer** | This is your moat — build it well |

The only part that's truly "yours to build" is the same Zammad/Jira integration you'd build in *every* option. Everything else is assembling mature open-source pieces with AI help.

## Realistic cost at 40 users (corrected)

| | Buy (Sirion) | Buy (LinkSquares) | **Build (assembled, AI-assisted)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year 1 | $200k–550k | $55k–175k | **~$5k–25k** |
| Ongoing/yr | $150k–350k | $40k–120k | **~$3k–18k** |
| 3-yr TCO | $1.0M–2.2M | $0.4M–1.0M | **~$15k–60k** |

Build ongoing cost = hosting (~$50–300/mo) + LLM API (~$100–1,000/mo depending on volume) + your AI tool subscriptions. At your scale this is **10–50× cheaper** than buying, and the asset is yours.

## What's actually hard (the real risk — it's not the code)

Cursor will write the code fast. These are what will actually bite you, so plan for them:

1. **Extraction accuracy on legal text.** "Good enough for 40 internal users" is achievable; flawless is not. Build a human-review step for low-confidence extractions.
2. **Security & access control for sensitive contracts.** This is non-negotiable and easy to get wrong. Get it right early.
3. **Bus factor — you're the sole maintainer.** When it breaks in 18 months, you own it. Keep the stack boring and well-documented.
4. **Your time is the scarce resource.** Cash cost is tiny, but founder hours spent building ≠ hours running the company. Time-box the MVP hard.

## Phased plan (so you ship value fast, not a 6-month science project)

1. **MVP (2–4 weeks):** Upload contracts → store → LLM extraction at ingestion → semantic search → manual ticket linking. Get yourself + a few users on it.
2. **Phase 2:** Automate the Zammad/Jira ticket-origin linkage (your differentiator).
3. **Phase 3:** Polish — dashboards, obligation reminders, refined extraction, access roles.

## The one question that changes this answer

**Are you building this only as an internal tool, or do you plan to sell it as a product later?**

- **Internal tool →** the build plan above is clearly best. Lock it in.
- **Productize / sell to other companies →** still build, but you'll need to invest much more in reliability, security, and multi-tenancy from day one — it becomes your core product, not a side tool. The economics still favor build, but the bar rises.

**Bottom line:** Given your scale, your tooling, and your ownership/revenue goals — **build it yourself by assembling open-source foundations + a custom Zammad/Jira layer + LLM-at-ingestion.** Just design for cheap API usage (embeddings for search, extract-once-and-cache), nail security, and time-box your MVP. That gives you the customizable, owned, revenue-friendly product you actually want — at roughly 1–5% of the cost of buying.

Want me to sketch the concrete MVP architecture and the repo/folder structure so you can start building it in Cursor right away?

The context has shifted—they're building this for their own 40-person company and plan to implement it themselves using AI coding tools, which dramatically changes the cost equation. They're pushing back on my implementation estimates because with Cursor and Claude, development costs are much lower, and as the owner, they want full control to customize the product themselves rather than paying recurring vendor fees.

I need to reassess honestly rather than just agreeing with what they want to hear. AI tools do slash build costs significantly, but they don't eliminate the hard parts—extraction quality, legal-grade reliability, security, compliance, and years of maintenance still require real work. For a 40-person internal tool, enterprise CLM pricing is probably overkill, but I'm still unclear whether this is meant to be an internal efficiency tool or something they'll eventually sell as a product.

There's a critical cost they're overlooking: building their own contract intelligence with Claude means they'll pay LLM API costs for every extraction and search operation—so they're not escaping API expenses, just shifting from vendor licensing to infrastructure costs. That's worth pointing out directly. They also need the integration layer with Zammad and Jira regardless of which path they choose.

Given the actual constraints—small scale, owner-built with AI assistance, desire for customization, aversion to recurring fees—the economics do shift. Enterprise CLM at $80k-400k annually is likely excessive for 40 users, whereas a self-built solution with modest LLM API usage and hosting could genuinely cost less, especially if contract volume stays reasonable. Their instinct toward building might actually be sound here.

The honest recommendation is to build smart rather than from scratch: assemble proven open-source components with a thin custom layer and LLM APIs for intelligence, rather than coding everything yourself. The real challenges aren't code volume—Cursor handles that—but extraction accuracy on legal documents, security controls for sensitive contracts, data migration, and the bus factor of being the sole maintainer. And there's the opportunity cost: founder time spent building is time not spent running the company.

For a phased MVP approach, I'd start with a contract repository linked to Zammad/Jira, add LLM-powered extraction and search, and control API costs through smart design: cache extractions, run LLM only at ingestion, use embeddings with a vector database for search so you're not hitting the LLM constantly, and pick appropriately-sized models. At 40 users, the all-in cost could be $200–1,500 monthly ($2.4k–18k yearly) for hosting, LLM APIs, and vector search—versus $30k–400k annually for enterprise CLM software. Over three years, you're looking at maybe $10k–60k total versus $400k–2.2M for buying, which is a massive difference at your scale. The caveat is that this assumes modest contract volume, you're comfortable being the maintainer, and "good enough" extraction quality rather than enterprise-grade perfection.

The smartest approach isn't building from scratch—it's assembling on an open-source foundation and layering AI-powered custom logic on top. One thing worth flagging: if you're planning to productize this and sell it, then enterprise-grade requirements come back into play and the build commitment gets bigger, though at that point it becomes your core product anyway so building still makes sense. The real risks aren't the code—they're extraction quality, maintaining the system, and whether your contract volume and compliance needs stay within what you can handle.

input.contents:
# IPD-1 — Should We Implement Our Own Legal Contract Intelligence Product?

> **Type:** Build-vs-Buy Decision Record
> **Decision area:** Legal Contract Intelligence Platform
> **Status:** Decided — **Build our own (AI-assisted)**
> **Date:** 2026-06-20
> **Owner:** [Company Owner]

---

## 1. Context

On review, **most requirements of the Legal Contract Intelligence Platform overlap with capabilities that mature CLM (Contract Lifecycle Management) vendors already ship well**:

- Document storage / repository
- Full-text and natural-language search
- Field and clause extraction
- Document-to-document linking

The **only genuine gap** is **reconstructing the originating context of a contract from our Zammad/Jira ticketing systems** — i.e., linking each contract back to the ticket/request that triggered it.

This decision record captures how we evaluated buying a CLM vs. building our own, and why the conclusion changed once our actual operating context (company size, tooling, and ownership goals) was made explicit.

---

## 2. Problem Statement

> Should we buy a CLM and integrate it into our intake system, or build our own Legal Contract Intelligence product?

---

## 3. Operating Constraints (Our Reality)

These facts are decisive and differentiate our situation from a generic enterprise buyer:

| Constraint | Value |
|---|---|
| Users | ~40 (internal: owner + company members) |
| Deployment | Internal tool (not initially sold as a product) |
| Build method | Self-implemented using **Cursor + Claude Code** (AI-assisted development) |
| Owner goals | Full customization, ownership of the asset, **revenue growth — not recurring vendor fees** |
| Differentiator to own | Zammad/Jira **ticket-origin** linkage |

---

## 4. Options Considered

### Option 1 — Buy Sirion + build a ticket-origin integration layer
Use Sirion for contract intelligence and repository capabilities; build a lightweight service to connect Zammad/Jira context into the contract lifecycle.

### Option 2 — Build a custom contract intelligence platform ourselves
Develop repository, extraction, retrieval, and relationship-management capabilities internally (assembled from open-source foundations + LLM APIs, not literally from scratch).

### Option 3 — Buy LinkSquares + build a ticket-origin integration layer
Use LinkSquares as the CLM foundation and extend it with custom integrations for ticket-origin tracking.

---

## 5. Cost Analysis

> CLM pricing is custom/opaque; figures below are 2026 benchmark ranges from public sources plus standard build-cost estimates. They are planning figures to validate against quotes, not exact quotes.

### 5.1 Buy-option pricing (benchmarks)

| Component | Sirion | LinkSquares |
|---|---|---|
| Annual license | $120k–$300k/yr | ~$30k–$120k/yr (median ~$31k; enterprise >$75k) |
| Implementation (one-time) | $100k–$250k+ | $25k–$100k |
| Support / maintenance | 18–22% of license/yr | +20–40% add-ons; API access ~$10k/yr |
| Integration layer (one-time) | ~$80k–$200k | ~$80k–$200k |
| Integration maintenance | ~$50k–$100k/yr | ~$50k–$100k/yr |

### 5.2 Three-year Total Cost of Ownership (at our 40-user scale)

| Option | 3-yr TCO |
|---|---|
| **Build (assembled, AI-assisted)** | **~$15k–$60k** |
| Buy LinkSquares + integration | ~$0.4M–$1.0M |
| Buy Sirion + integration | ~$1.0M–$2.2M |

### 5.3 Why build is so much cheaper *for us*

- **Labor cost collapses**: Cursor + Claude Code replace a paid engineering team. Cost shifts to the owner's time + AI tool subscriptions.
- **Scale is small**: enterprise CLM licenses are priced for large orgs; at 40 seats we'd pay enterprise rates for reliability/capability we don't need.
- **No recurring license drain**: a license is pure cost outflow every year; a built asset is a mostly one-time investment plus low running costs.

### 5.4 Important correction — building does *not* eliminate API cost

A CLM's "intelligence" runs on LLM calls. Building our own **shifts** the spend from a vendor license to **our own LLM API + hosting bill** — it does not remove it.

The real question is therefore *"is our own API + hosting bill smaller than a CLM license?"* — and at our scale, **yes, dramatically**, provided we design for cheap usage:

- Use **embeddings + a vector store (e.g., pgvector)** for search so we are *not* calling an LLM on every query.
- Run **LLM extraction once at ingestion and cache the result**, not repeatedly.
- Choose appropriately sized models per task.

Estimated build running cost at our scale: **~$200–$1,500/month** (hosting + API + AI subscriptions) ≈ **$3k–$18k/yr**, vs **$40k–$400k/yr** to buy.

---

## 6. Decision

**We will build our own Legal Contract Intelligence product (Option 2)**, assembled from proven open-source foundations + a custom Zammad/Jira ticket-origin layer + LLM-at-ingestion for intelligence.

### Ownership rationale

> I believe this shows ownership skills, since X's suggestion of Sirion as the base is efficient in the development stage, but in the long term it is not cost-efficient. Also, considering our company's requirements, I came to the conclusion that building it on our own will be the better option.

**Expanded reasoning:**
- **Short-term vs. long-term trade-off recognized:** Buying (Sirion/LinkSquares) is faster to stand up initially, but its recurring license + implementation cost is not cost-efficient over a multi-year horizon for a 40-user internal tool.
- **Fit to actual requirements:** Most CLM features are commodity; the only differentiated need (ticket-origin linkage) must be custom-built under *every* option anyway.
- **Ownership & revenue alignment:** Building yields a fully owned, customizable asset whose cost grows with usage (variable) rather than with vendor pricing (fixed, escalating) — aligning spend with revenue rather than draining earnings on licenses.
- **Tooling makes it feasible:** AI-assisted development (Cursor + Claude Code) removes the historical labor barrier that previously made "buy" the default.

---

## 7. Risks & Mitigations

The hard part is **not the code** (AI handles that) — it is the following:

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Extraction accuracy on legal text | Human-review step for low-confidence extractions; iterate on prompts/models |
| Security & access control for sensitive contracts | Design access roles and encryption early; treat as non-negotiable |
| Sole-maintainer / bus factor | Keep the stack boring and well-documented; minimize exotic dependencies |
| Owner time = scarce resource | Time-box the MVP; ship incrementally |
| Uncontrolled LLM API cost | Embeddings for search, extract-once-and-cache, right-size models |

---

## 8. Implementation Approach (Phased)

| Phase | Scope | Target |
|---|---|---|
| **MVP** | Upload → store → LLM extraction at ingestion → semantic search → manual ticket linking | 2–4 weeks |
| **Phase 2** | Automate Zammad/Jira ticket-origin linkage (the differentiator) | Next |
| **Phase 3** | Dashboards, obligation reminders, refined extraction, access roles | Later |

**Reference stack (to be assembled, not written from scratch):**
- Storage: Postgres + object storage (S3 / local)
- Search: pgvector (embeddings)
- Extraction: LLM API at ingestion, cached
- Linking: embeddings similarity + metadata
- Ticket-origin: custom integration service (Zammad/Jira) — *our core value*

---

## 9. Open Question (could revise scope, not the decision)

**Is this strictly an internal tool, or will it later be sold as a product?**
- *Internal tool* → the plan above stands as-is.
- *Productize / sell externally* → still build, but raise the bar on reliability, security, and multi-tenancy from day one; it becomes a core product rather than a side tool.

---

## 10. Summary

For a **40-user internal tool**, built with **AI-assisted development**, by an **owner who values customization, asset ownership, and revenue alignment**, **building our own** is **~10–50× cheaper over three years** than buying a CLM, delivers full control over the one differentiated capability (ticket-origin linkage), and avoids perpetual license fees — at the cost of owning extraction quality, security, and maintenance ourselves, which are manageable at this scale.

content.text:
I've documented the full decision at `IPD-1-build-vs-buy-decision.md`. It's structured as a proper build-vs-buy decision record so it stands on its own for anyone reviewing it later.

What it captures:

- **Context & problem statement** — the commodity-vs-gap framing (ticket-origin linkage is the only real gap).
- **Operating constraints** — your actual reality (40 users, internal, Cursor + Claude Code, ownership/revenue goals), which is what drives the decision.
- **The three options** — Sirion, build, LinkSquares.
- **Cost analysis** — benchmark pricing, the 3-year TCO table (build ~$15k–60k vs Sirion ~$1–2.2M), *and* the honest correction that building shifts API cost to you rather than eliminating it (with the design choices that keep it cheap).
- **The decision + your ownership rationale** — your exact quote is included verbatim under "Ownership rationale," with expanded reasoning that frames it as a deliberate short-term-vs-long-term trade-off (which is what demonstrates the ownership thinking).

I believe this shows ownership skills because I didn't take the "safe buy" answer at face value — I pressure-tested it against my actual context and made a call I can defend.

Here's my reasoning:

When I first looked at this, the obvious recommendation was to buy a mature CLM (Sirion or LinkSquares) and build only a thin integration layer, since most requirements — storage, search, extraction, document linking — are commodity features vendors already ship well. The only real gap is reconstructing a contract's originating context from our Zammad/Jira tickets.

But I challenged that recommendation against three facts that actually apply to *us*:

1. **Scale:** We're 40 internal users, not an enterprise. Enterprise CLM tools price for large orgs — I'd be paying $30k–400k a year for reliability and capacity we don't need.

2. **Tooling:** I'm building with Cursor and Claude Code, so the labor cost that normally justifies "buy over build" collapses. The build math that's true for a hired team is not true for me.

3. **Ownership and revenue:** A license is a recurring drain on money we've earned. I want an asset I own and can customize — one that improves our margins instead of adding a permanent cost line.

So I'm choosing to **build it myself** — but deliberately by *assembling* proven open-source foundations (Postgres, pgvector for search, object storage) plus an LLM API used only at document ingestion, with the Zammad/Jira ticket-origin layer as the custom piece that's genuinely ours. At our scale this runs roughly 1–5% of the cost of buying.

I also caught the trap in my own thinking: building doesn't eliminate API costs, it just shifts them from a vendor license to my own LLM bill — so I've planned the architecture (embeddings for search, extract-once-and-cache) specifically to keep that bill small.

I'm not asking permission to proceed — I've done the homework and I'm confident in the direction.
